sourceTitle: Eliminate email busywork with Notion Mail best practices

questions.question: Which Notion Mail feature automatically groups similar incoming emails for you?

options.text: Auto label
options.explanation: Auto label uses Notion AI to sort similar emails into dedicated views, automating organization.

options.text: Smart folders
options.explanation: Smart folders are not a Notion Mail feature mentioned in the guide.

options.text: Email filters
options.explanation: Traditional email filters exist but the guide emphasizes the AI‑driven Auto label.

options.text: Tag clustering
options.explanation: Tag clustering is not a specific Notion Mail capability discussed.

questions.question: Where can you find all of your saved snippets while composing an email?

options.text: In the left‑hand navigation panel
options.explanation: Snippets are not stored in the left navigation.

options.text: In the settings menu on the right side of the screen
options.explanation: The guide shows a screenshot of snippets accessed via the right‑hand settings menu.

options.text: At the top of the email composer
options.explanation: The top of the composer holds formatting tools, not the snippet library.

options.text: In a separate Notion page linked from the email
options.explanation: Snippets are managed within the mail interface, not an external page.

questions.question: Which icon do you click to insert a scheduling link into an email?

options.text: Paperclip icon
options.explanation: The paperclip attaches files, not scheduling links.

options.text: Calendar icon
options.explanation: The guide states that clicking the calendar icon creates a scheduling link.

options.text: Lightning bolt icon
options.explanation: There is no lightning bolt icon mentioned for scheduling.

options.text: Clock icon
options.explanation: The clock icon is not described as the scheduling‑link trigger.

questions.question: How does the “Group by” option improve the way you view emails in Notion Mail?

options.text: It automatically deletes low‑priority emails.
options.explanation: Grouping does not delete emails; it only arranges them.

options.text: It lets you organize emails into visual groups based on a selected property.
options.explanation: Group by lets you pick a property (e.g., priority) and displays emails in grouped sections, matching your workflow.

options.text: It merges duplicate messages into a single thread.
options.explanation: Merging duplicates is not a function of Group by.

options.text: It creates a separate Notion page for each email.
options.explanation: Group by only changes how emails appear within the current view.

questions.question: According to Meighan, what is a good guideline for the number of inbox views you should create?

options.text: Create as many as possible to cover every scenario.
options.explanation: Meighan warns against having too many views.

options.text: Keep views actionable and avoid excessive numbers (don’t go nuts with 75).
options.explanation: She advises focusing on a few actionable views rather than dozens.

options.text: Only use the default view provided by Notion Mail.
options.explanation: Customization is encouraged; the default alone isn’t enough.

options.text: Create one view per client.
options.explanation: While possible, this can quickly lead to view overload, which Meighan cautions against.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly describes how to create a new snippet in the email composer?

options.text: Click the calendar icon → select a time slot → save as snippet.
options.explanation: The calendar icon creates scheduling links, not snippets.

options.text: Click the {} snippet button → select New snippet → give a title → type content → Save.
options.explanation: These are the exact steps outlined for building a snippet.

options.text: Press / → choose ‘Snippet’ block → write → auto‑save.
options.explanation: The slash command adds blocks, not the snippet creation workflow.

options.text: Open settings → go to Snippets tab → click Add New → fill form.
options.explanation: Snippets are accessed from the composer, not directly via settings for creation.

questions.question: Why does the guide suggest writing subject lines “like SEO”?

options.text: To improve email deliverability and avoid spam filters.
options.explanation: SEO‑style subject lines focus on discoverability, not spam avoidance.

options.text: Because clear, searchable subject lines make it easier to find important emails later.
options.explanation: Treating subject lines like SEO helps users locate messages quickly.

options.text: To automatically rank emails in Notion’s search results.
options.explanation: Notion doesn’t rank emails like web pages; the analogy is about clarity.

options.text: So that subject lines can be used as hashtags.
options.explanation: Hashtags aren’t mentioned as a purpose for SEO‑style subjects.

questions.question: How does Notion AI assist when drafting a new email or snippet?

options.text: It automatically sends the email after you type a single word.
options.explanation: AI helps generate content, not send messages automatically.

options.text: Pressing space after creating a canvas opens the AI writer, which can suggest or generate text.
options.explanation: The guide explains that the spacebar opens the AI writer for drafting assistance.

options.text: It creates a separate Notion page for the email content.
options.explanation: AI works within the composer, not by generating a new page.

options.text: It adds a random emoji to each paragraph.
options.explanation: AI’s role is to help with writing, not to insert random emojis.

questions.question: Explain how combining auto‑labeling with dedicated views improves Meighan’s productivity.

options.text: Auto‑labeling groups similar emails, and dedicated views let her focus on specific categories like payments or testimonials, reducing manual sorting and enabling quick access to high‑value items.
options.explanation: This captures the workflow: AI labels, then views isolate priority streams, saving time.

options.text: Auto‑labeling automatically replies to all emails, and dedicated views archive them.
options.explanation: Auto‑labeling does not send replies; it only organizes.

options.text: Dedicated views replace the need for any labeling, making AI unnecessary.
options.explanation: Views rely on labels to filter; they work together, not as alternatives.

options.text: Auto‑labeling creates separate Notion databases for each client, which are then displayed in dedicated views.
options.explanation: Labels operate within the same inbox, not by spawning new databases.

questions.question: Compare Dave’s priority‑based view setup with Meighan’s AI‑driven labeling system. Which statement best describes a key difference?

options.text: Dave manually drags emails into priority categories within a view, while Meighan relies on Notion AI to automatically assign labels and populate dedicated views.
options.explanation: Dave’s workflow is hands‑on priority sorting; Meighan leverages AI auto‑labeling for automatic categorization.

options.text: Both Dave and Meighan use the same auto‑label feature; the only difference is the color scheme of their views.
options.explanation: Dave’s method is described as manual priority dragging, not auto‑label.

options.text: Dave uses snippets for every email, whereas Meighan never uses snippets.
options.explanation: Both consultants heavily use snippets; this isn’t the primary distinction.

options.text: Dave’s system requires a separate Notion page for each priority, while Meighan’s uses a single page with filters.
options.explanation: The guide does not mention separate pages; the difference lies in manual vs. AI sorting.
